nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated, benthic; inhabits coral reefs and rocky shorelines at depths of 0–35 m

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on fish, molluscs, and crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, has been reported to accept squid, octopus, and meaty marine foods; live feeder fish may be needed initially to stimulate a feeding response. Generally fed every 2–4 days to satiation. Feeding with tongs or a feeding stick is generally advised to avoid accidental bites.

avoidWith: fish small enough to be swallowed

summary: The Fire Coral Eel (Gymnothorax miliaris) is a medium-to-large moray eel from Atlantic reefs, recognized by its dark body scattered with golden spots. Generally considered an intermediate-level species, it is widely regarded as an escape specialist that requires a tight-fitting, gap-free lid and a large aquarium of at least 600 liters.
careNotes:
The Fire Coral Eel is a medium-to-large moray eel that tends to spend much of its time concealed among live rock, extending its head from a crevice or ledge. It is widely regarded as an escape specialist, and a tightly-fitted, gap-free lid is considered essential. Given that adults can reach up to 67 cm, a minimum aquarium volume of around 600 liters (with larger being preferable) is generally advised. Some individuals have been known to become less reclusive over time and will rest visibly in the open.

The Fire Coral Eel is generally regarded as semi-aggressive, and as a predatory eel it will consume anything small enough to fit in its mouth. Crustaceans, shrimp, and small fish tankmates are broadly considered at risk, and keeping this species in an invertebrate-heavy reef tank is generally approached with caution. Conspecific aggression has been reported when multiple individuals are housed together; it tends to be a solitary species in the wild.

The trade name 'Golden Banana Moray Eel' or 'Banana Eel' refers to a rare, fully yellow color morph of the same species, Gymnothorax miliaris, which commands premium pricing. The typical wild-type form is dark brown to black with scattered golden spots. The 'Banana Eel' trade name is sometimes applied to unrelated species as well, so confirming the scientific name before purchase is advisable.

Strong biological filtration and a protein skimmer are generally advisable given the species' high-protein diet and the resultant waste load.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented for this species in the aquarium hobby. In the wild, Gymnothorax miliaris is oviparous (egg-laying), but the conditions required to induce reproductive behavior in a home aquarium have not been established.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary product page for the Fire Coral Eel (standard dark morph). A second LiveAquaria page for the Golden Banana Moray Eel (yellow morph, same species) was also consulted at https://www.liveaquaria.com/product/1423/?pcatid=1423.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Muraenidae / Anguilliformes), native range (Western + Eastern Atlantic), max size (70 cm), and temperature data (24.6–28°C). Also confirmed IUCN: Least Concern.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Provided additional water parameter values: 76–80°F, SG 1.023–1.026, dKH 8–12, pH 7.8–8.4. Used in averaging temperature, salinity, and pH.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed 70 cm max size; recommended minimum tank of 800 L (outlier vs. LiveAquaria 473 L and FishIndex 530 L; all three averaged to ~601 L). Noted reef-safe-with-caution designation.

additional.name: FishIndex
additional.notes: Provided additional tank size reference (140 gal / 530 L); confirmed carnivore diet and escape-risk behavior. Listed as reef-unsafe; contributed to reef-safe disagreement noted in careNotes.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. The species is oviparous in the wild.
